name: Protocol Split (A2A / MCP)
summary: Two complementary protocols: one for tool access (MCP), one for agent-to-agent discovery and delegation (A2A).
description: A two-layer interoperability architecture separating how agents access tools from how agents find and communicate with each other. MCP (Model Context Protocol) standardizes tool, API, and data access -- the agent-to-infrastructure layer. A2A (Agent-to-Agent) standardizes discovery, delegation, and status reporting between peer agents -- the agent-to-agent layer. The separation lets each protocol evolve independently and prevents tool-access concerns from contaminating peer communication semantics. Both are now governed by the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation.
realWorldExample: A customer service agent discovers a billing specialist via A2A's agent card mechanism, delegates a refund investigation, and polls for completion. The billing specialist uses MCP to query the payment API, generate a refund, and report results back through A2A.
whenToUse: When building multi-agent systems that need to interoperate across organizational or platform boundaries, or when agents need to discover and delegate to peers they weren't specifically designed to work with.
strengths: Clean separation of concerns between tool access and peer communication
Both protocols are open standards with broad industry backing
Agents can be added to a system without modifying existing agents
Discovery mechanism allows dynamic team composition
weaknesses: Two protocols to implement and maintain instead of one
A2A discovery relies on agent cards that must be kept current
Latency overhead of protocol negotiation for simple agent interactions
Standards are still maturing -- breaking changes possible

agents.name: Requesting Agent
agents.description: Discovers peers via A2A agent cards, delegates tasks, and polls for results

agents.name: Specialist Agent
agents.description: Publishes an agent card describing capabilities, accepts delegated tasks via A2A, uses MCP to access tools

agents.name: Agent Registry
agents.description: Hosts agent cards for discovery, enabling dynamic team composition

shared: Agent card registry with capability descriptions
private: Task state and delegation history per requesting agent
memoryArchitecture.description: Registry is the shared discovery layer; agents maintain private task context. MCP tool access is stateless.

diagram:
graph TD
  R[Requesting Agent] -->|A2A: discover| REG[Agent Registry]
  REG -->|agent cards| R
  R -->|A2A: delegate task| S[Specialist Agent]
  S -->|MCP: use tools| T[Tools / APIs]
  S -->|A2A: report result| R

name: Event-Driven Production Stack
summary: Three-layer production architecture: durable event backbone, agent delegation protocol, and tool access protocol.
description: A composite architecture for production multi-agent systems combining three layers: a durable event backbone (e.g., Kafka) for reliable message delivery and replay, A2A for inter-agent delegation and discovery, and MCP for tool access. Events flow through topics, orchestrator agents consume and delegate via A2A, specialist agents use MCP to interact with external systems, and results publish back to the event backbone. The event log provides durability, auditability, and replay capability that pure agent-to-agent communication lacks.
realWorldExample: An MLOps pipeline where model training completion events flow through Kafka, an orchestrator agent picks them up via A2A delegation to evaluation agents, which use MCP to access metrics APIs, and publish pass/fail results back to Kafka for downstream deployment agents.
whenToUse: When agent systems need production-grade reliability: durable message delivery, event replay for debugging, audit trails, and decoupled scaling of producers and consumers.
strengths: Durable event log enables replay, debugging, and audit
Decoupled producers and consumers scale independently
Clean separation across three well-defined layers
Failed agents can restart and resume from the event log
weaknesses: Significant infrastructure complexity (Kafka cluster + protocol servers)
Latency of event serialization and deserialization at each layer
Overkill for small systems with a few agents
Debugging spans three abstraction layers

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: Consumes events from the backbone, delegates tasks to specialists via A2A

agents.name: Specialist Worker
agents.description: Receives delegated tasks, uses MCP to access tools, publishes results back to the event backbone

agents.name: Event Monitor
agents.description: Watches the event backbone for anomalies, dead letters, and SLA violations

shared: Durable event log (Kafka topics) as system of record
private: Orchestrator maintains delegation state; monitor tracks SLA metrics
memoryArchitecture.description: The event backbone is the shared memory layer. Individual agents are ephemeral and can be replaced without data loss.

diagram:
graph TD
  K[Event Backbone] -->|consume| O[Orchestrator]
  O -->|A2A delegate| S1[Specialist 1]
  O -->|A2A delegate| S2[Specialist 2]
  S1 -->|MCP| T1[Tools]
  S2 -->|MCP| T2[APIs]
  S1 -->|publish result| K
  S2 -->|publish result| K
  K -->|watch| M[Event Monitor]

name: Corporate Hierarchy with Budget Caps
summary: CEO-Manager-Worker hierarchy where each agent has a budget cap and humans serve as the board of directors.
description: A hierarchical multi-agent system modeled as a corporate org chart. A CEO agent receives top-level goals and decomposes them into tasks delegated to Manager agents, who spawn and coordinate ephemeral Worker agents. Every agent has an explicit budget cap (tokens, API calls, or cost) that it cannot exceed. Humans serve as the board of directors -- agents cannot hire new agents, change strategy, or exceed budget without human approval. The corporate metaphor is taken literally as a runtime model: agents have titles, reporting lines, and resource constraints enforced by the system.
realWorldExample: Paperclip framework (44K+ GitHub stars, March 2026): a CEO agent coordinates Managers who spawn Workers. Each has a budget. Humans approve new hires and strategy changes.
whenToUse: When you need cost-controlled autonomous agent systems with clear human oversight, especially when tasks involve spending real money (API calls, cloud resources) and you want hard limits.
strengths: Budget caps prevent runaway costs at every level of the hierarchy
Human-as-board provides natural oversight without micromanagement
Dynamic agent spawning adapts team size to task complexity
Corporate metaphor is intuitive for non-technical stakeholders
weaknesses: Budget tracking adds overhead to every agent action
Rigid hierarchy may bottleneck at manager level
Human approval gates can stall autonomous operation
Corporate metaphor may constrain creative agent coordination

agents.name: CEO
agents.description: Receives top-level goals, decomposes into strategies, delegates to managers

agents.name: Manager
agents.description: Coordinates a domain, spawns and supervises ephemeral workers within its budget

agents.name: Worker
agents.description: Ephemeral agent spawned for a specific task, operates within budget cap, reports to manager

agents.name: Board (Human)
agents.description: Approves new agent creation, strategy changes, and budget increases

shared: Budget ledger tracking spend per agent, org chart with reporting lines
private: CEO maintains strategy state; managers track worker assignments
memoryArchitecture.description: Budget is the central coordination mechanism. Every agent action is tracked against its budget cap.

diagram:
graph TD
  B[Board - Human] -->|approve| CEO[CEO Agent]
  CEO -->|delegate| M1[Manager 1]
  CEO -->|delegate| M2[Manager 2]
  M1 -->|spawn| W1[Worker]
  M1 -->|spawn| W2[Worker]
  M2 -->|spawn| W3[Worker]
  W1 -->|report| M1
  M1 -->|report| CEO
  BL[Budget Ledger] -.->|enforce| CEO
  BL -.->|enforce| M1
  BL -.->|enforce| W1

name: Memory Distillation Pipeline
summary: Raw conversation is progressively compressed into durable memory tiers during active operation.
description: A staged pipeline that continuously distills agent interactions into progressively more durable and compact forms: raw conversation logs are compressed into session notes, session notes are consolidated into global notes and profile updates. Unlike sleep/wake consolidation cycles, this runs during active operation. A distiller agent monitors conversation accumulation and triggers compression when thresholds are reached. The resulting memory tiers -- episodic (what happened), semantic (what was learned), and procedural (how to do it) -- use different storage and retrieval strategies optimized for their access patterns.
realWorldExample: OpenAI Agents SDK's context personalization: a state-based memory store maintains a structured user profile and notes, distills memories during runs, and consolidates session notes into global notes at session end. AWS AgentCore implements tiered memory with automatic distillation across episodic, semantic, and procedural layers.
whenToUse: When agents run long sessions that accumulate context, when you need cross-session memory without keeping raw transcripts, or when memory retrieval speed matters and different memory types have different access patterns.
strengths: Keeps working context lean by continuously compressing
Different retrieval strategies per memory tier optimize for access patterns
Cross-session continuity without storing raw transcripts
Progressive compression naturally prioritizes important information
weaknesses: Distillation is lossy -- subtle context may be dropped
Three-tier architecture is more complex than flat memory files
Distiller agent consumes tokens that could go to primary work
Tier boundaries can be fuzzy (is this episodic or semantic?)

agents.name: Primary Agent
agents.description: Performs the main task, generates raw conversation and working context

agents.name: Distiller
agents.description: Monitors context accumulation, compresses raw conversation into structured notes at threshold triggers

agents.name: Consolidator
agents.description: At session boundaries, merges session notes into global notes and profile updates

memoryArchitecture.description: Three tiers: ephemeral working context, session-scoped episodic notes, durable semantic/procedural memory. Distillation flows one direction: raw → session → global.

diagram:
graph LR
  R[Raw Conversation] -->|threshold trigger| D[Distiller]
  D -->|compress| SN[Session Notes]
  SN -->|session end| C[Consolidator]
  C -->|merge| GM[Global Memory]
  GM -->|retrieve| P[Primary Agent]

name: Metacognitive Self-Improvement Loop
summary: Three-component metacognitive loop: self-assess capabilities, plan learning, evaluate learning outcomes.
description: Inspired by educational psychology, this pattern structures agent self-improvement around three metacognitive components that execute within a single recurrence cycle without fine-tuning. Metacognitive Knowledge: the agent assesses its own capabilities, identifying what it can and cannot do well. Metacognitive Planning: based on that self-assessment, the agent decides what to learn and how (which examples to study, which strategies to try). Metacognitive Evaluation: after attempting the learning, the agent reflects on whether the learning improved performance and adjusts its learning strategy accordingly. The loop produces rapid self-evolution within a single session.
realWorldExample: MARS framework (2026 paper): an agent self-assesses its math reasoning capability, identifies that it struggles with geometric proofs, selects relevant examples to study, attempts similar problems, evaluates whether its approach improved, and adjusts its strategy -- all within one inference cycle.
whenToUse: When agents need to improve at a task during execution without fine-tuning, when you want structured reflection that goes beyond simple retry-on-failure, or when task domains require adaptive learning strategies.
strengths: Self-improvement without any model fine-tuning
Three-component structure prevents unfocused reflection
Single-cycle execution -- improvement happens immediately
Learning strategy itself improves over iterations
weaknesses: Self-assessment can be inaccurate -- agents may misjudge their capabilities
Uses significant token budget on introspection rather than task execution
Effectiveness depends on quality of self-assessment and planning
May not generalize improvements to dissimilar tasks

agents.name: Self-Assessor
agents.description: Evaluates the agent's current capabilities and identifies weaknesses

agents.name: Learning Planner
agents.description: Designs a learning strategy based on identified weaknesses: what to study, which strategies to try

agents.name: Executor
agents.description: Attempts the task using the planned learning strategy

agents.name: Learning Evaluator
agents.description: Reflects on whether the learning strategy improved performance and adjusts future planning

private: Capability model per assessor, learning strategies per planner
memoryArchitecture.description: The evaluation feedback loop is the key memory: it informs future self-assessments and learning plans. Executor is ephemeral -- only the learning outcome persists.

diagram:
graph TD
  A[Self-Assessor] -->|weaknesses| P[Learning Planner]
  P -->|strategy| E[Executor]
  E -->|results| V[Learning Evaluator]
  V -->|meta-feedback| A
  V -->|adjust strategy| P

name: Experiential Heuristic Library
summary: Agents extract reusable heuristics from both successes and failures into a persistent pool retrieved at task time.
description: During task execution, a reflection agent analyzes outcomes -- both successes and failures -- to extract positive heuristics: concise, reusable rules of thumb (e.g., 'when parsing nested JSON, validate depth first'). These are stored in a persistent heuristic pool with metadata about the context in which they were learned. At test time for new tasks, relevant heuristics are retrieved via embedding similarity and injected into the working agent's context. Unlike Reflexion (which stores verbal reflections of failures only), this builds a reusable experience library from all outcomes.
realWorldExample: ERL framework (2026 paper): a coding agent attempts a task, succeeds, and the reflector extracts 'use itertools.groupby for consecutive-element problems.' Later, when a new task involves grouping, the heuristic is retrieved and injected into the prompt.
whenToUse: When agents repeatedly encounter similar task types and could benefit from accumulated experience, when you want learning without fine-tuning, or when you need to transfer knowledge between agent instances.
strengths: Learns from successes, not just failures
Heuristics transfer between different agent instances
Embedding-based retrieval finds relevant experience even for novel tasks
Library grows more valuable over time
weaknesses: Heuristic quality depends on the reflector's judgment
Stale or domain-specific heuristics can mislead in new contexts
Embedding similarity may retrieve superficially relevant but wrong heuristics
Pool requires periodic pruning to avoid context bloat

agents.name: Task Agent
agents.description: Performs the primary task with heuristics injected into its context

agents.name: Reflector
agents.description: Analyzes task outcomes and extracts reusable heuristics

agents.name: Heuristic Librarian
agents.description: Maintains the heuristic pool, handles retrieval by embedding similarity, and prunes stale entries

shared: Heuristic pool with embeddings and provenance metadata
private: Reflector maintains extraction templates and quality criteria
memoryArchitecture.description: The heuristic pool is the core shared memory. Each heuristic includes the context it was learned in, an embedding for retrieval, and a confidence score that decays if the heuristic leads to failures.

diagram:
graph TD
  TA[Task Agent] -->|outcome| R[Reflector]
  R -->|extract heuristic| L[Heuristic Librarian]
  L -->|retrieve relevant| TA
  L -->|prune stale| L

name: Fractal Modularity (MoE × MoA × Multi-Agent)
summary: Nested composition of MoE, MoA, and multi-agent systems using the same decompose-specialize-recompose pattern at every level.
description: A hierarchical architecture where the same principle -- decompose, route to specialists, recompose results -- is applied at three nested levels. At the token level, Mixture-of-Experts routes individual tokens to specialized neural network submodules. At the model level, Mixture-of-Adapters routes tasks to specialized fine-tuned model variants. At the system level, Multi-Agent orchestration routes subtasks to specialized agent roles. Each level uses the same decompose-specialize-recompose principle but operates at a different granularity. The fractal nature means insights from optimizing one level (e.g., routing strategies) transfer to other levels.
realWorldExample: A research system where: the base model uses MoE for efficient token processing, domain-adapted LoRA adapters are selected per query type (legal, medical, technical), and a multi-agent system coordinates between research, fact-checking, and synthesis agents -- all using router-based specialization.
whenToUse: When building large-scale systems that need specialization at multiple granularities, when routing strategy insights should transfer across levels, or when you want a unified conceptual framework for heterogeneous AI composition.
strengths: Single mental model applies at every level of the system
Routing optimizations at one level inform others
Extreme specialization at every granularity
Modular -- each level can be swapped independently
weaknesses: Extremely complex to implement all three levels
Routing errors compound across levels
Few teams have the expertise to optimize across all three granularities
Theoretical elegance may not justify practical complexity

agents.name: System Router
agents.description: Top-level orchestrator that decomposes tasks and routes to specialized agent teams

agents.name: Model Selector
agents.description: Within each agent, selects the appropriate model adapter or variant for the current subtask

agents.name: Specialist Agent
agents.description: Executes specialized work using a model with task-appropriate MoE routing and adapter selection

shared: Routing performance metrics across all three levels
private: Model selector maintains adapter performance history
memoryArchitecture.description: The key insight is tracking routing quality at each level. Performance data flows up: MoE stats inform adapter selection, adapter stats inform agent routing.

diagram:
graph TD
  SR[System Router] -->|route task| A1[Agent: Research]
  SR -->|route task| A2[Agent: Analysis]
  A1 -->|select adapter| MS1[Model Selector]
  MS1 -->|legal LoRA| M1[MoE Model]
  A2 -->|select adapter| MS2[Model Selector]
  MS2 -->|medical LoRA| M2[MoE Model]
  A1 -->|results| SR
  A2 -->|results| SR

name: Diversity-Preserving Agent Ensemble
summary: Agent ensemble that actively prevents homogenization by maximizing output diversity via embedding distance or persona assignment.
description: A refinement of Mixture-of-Agents that solves the homogenization problem: when multiple agents discuss the same topic, they tend to converge on similar outputs, defeating the purpose of having multiple perspectives. Two mechanisms combat this. RMoA (Ranked Mixture-of-Agents) uses embedding-based greedy selection to maximize cosine distance between chosen agent outputs, ensuring the ensemble captures the widest range of perspectives. SMoA (Specialized Mixture-of-Agents) assigns each agent an explicit persona or role description that forces divergent reasoning approaches. Both can be combined.
realWorldExample: A code review ensemble where one agent reviews for security (persona: 'paranoid security auditor'), another for performance ('systems performance engineer'), and another for readability ('junior developer onboarding'). SMoA personas force genuinely different perspectives rather than three similar reviews.
whenToUse: When you need multiple genuinely different perspectives rather than redundant agreement, when vanilla MoA produces homogeneous outputs, or when the aggregator needs diverse inputs to make better decisions.
strengths: Produces genuinely diverse perspectives instead of redundant agreement
Embedding-based selection is automatic and objective
Persona assignment is simple to implement and intuitive
Better aggregated decisions from truly diverse inputs
weaknesses: Diversity for its own sake can include low-quality outlier perspectives
Embedding distance doesn't guarantee semantic quality
Persona assignment requires careful design to avoid caricatures
More computation per ensemble step than vanilla MoA

agents.name: Diverse Agent
agents.description: Produces output from a specific persona or reasoning approach, maximizing divergence from peers

agents.name: Diversity Selector
agents.description: Ranks outputs by pairwise cosine distance and selects maximally diverse subset

agents.name: Aggregator
agents.description: Synthesizes the diverse outputs into a final result that reflects the full range of perspectives

shared: Persona definitions for SMoA, embedding space for RMoA selection
private: Aggregator tracks which persona combinations produce best results
memoryArchitecture.description: Agents are ephemeral and stateless. The diversity mechanism (embedding distance or persona library) is the coordination layer.

diagram:
graph TD
  P[Persona Library] -->|assign| A1[Agent: Security]
  P -->|assign| A2[Agent: Performance]
  P -->|assign| A3[Agent: Readability]
  A1 -->|output| DS[Diversity Selector]
  A2 -->|output| DS
  A3 -->|output| DS
  DS -->|max-distance subset| AG[Aggregator]
  AG -->|synthesized result| OUT[Final Output]
